_meta.keyKnowledge: We develop project management plans for intended purposes and audiences to manage the creation of designed solutions individually, in teams and collaboratively.

toLevel2.question: A project management plan consists only of a list of materials for the project, with no reference to tasks, timelines, responsibilities or objectives in any phase.
toLevel2.correct: False
toLevel2.correctExplanation: A project management plan integrates objectives, deliverables, tasks, timelines, resources and responsibilities. A materials list alone is insufficient.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: A plan integrates multiple elements beyond a materials list.

toLevel2.question: A milestone in a project is a specific checkpoint marking the completion of a defined deliverable or phase rather than routine daily activity.
toLevel2.correct: True
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Milestones mark completion of defined deliverables or phases, distinguishing them from routine daily activity.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Milestones mark specific completions rather than routine activity.

toLevel2.question: A Gantt chart is a pie chart showing the proportion of budget allocated to each task, with no timeline or task-duration information included in the visualisation.
toLevel2.correct: False
toLevel2.correctExplanation: A Gantt chart is a timeline-based schedule with tasks shown as horizontal bars along a timeline; pie-chart budget visualisation is a different tool entirely.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Gantt charts are timelines rather than budget pie charts.

toLevel2.question: Risk assessment in a project plan identifies potential problems that could affect the project and outlines responses to reduce or manage those problems if they occur.
toLevel2.correct: True
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Risk assessment identifies potential problems and planned responses to manage them.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Risk assessment identifies potential problems and planned responses.

toLevel2.question: A project can proceed to completion without any allocation of resources — time, people, money, materials — as long as the designer has a clear vision of the outcome.
toLevel2.correct: False
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Resources — time, people, money, materials — are essential to project completion; vision alone does not deliver outcomes.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Resources are essential; vision alone does not deliver outcomes.

toLevel2.question: A project budget is a single lump sum transferred at completion with no prior planning about how money will be distributed across tasks, resources or contingencies.
toLevel2.correct: False
toLevel2.correctExplanation: A budget plans distribution of money or value units across tasks, resources and contingencies in advance; lump-sum transfer at completion is not a budget.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: A budget plans distribution across project elements rather than representing a single lump sum at completion.

toLevel2.question: Stakeholders in a project are only the paying clients, with no role for team members, end users, teachers or community members whose interests the project may affect.
toLevel2.correct: False
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Stakeholders include anyone affected by or contributing to the project — team, users, teachers, community — not paying clients alone.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Stakeholders include team, users, teachers and community alongside any paying clients.

toLevel2.question: Task dependencies require that two tasks always use identical resources and identical personnel, with no other form of relationship between the two tasks in any project.
toLevel2.correct: False
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Task dependencies describe sequence relationships where one task's progress constrains another's start or finish; they are not about identical resources or personnel.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Dependencies concern sequence rather than identical resources or personnel.

toLevel2.question: Post-project review is a stage in which the team reflects on what worked and what to improve for future projects, capturing lessons for transfer.
toLevel2.correct: True
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Post-project review captures lessons for transfer to future projects through structured reflection.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Review captures transferable lessons through reflection on what worked and what to improve.

toLevel2.question: A quality requirement specifies the standard a deliverable must meet, such as tolerances, material properties, performance criteria or user-experience measures.
toLevel2.correct: True
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Quality requirements set standards deliverables must meet across several possible dimensions.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Quality requirements define specific standards a deliverable must meet.

toLevel2.question: Once a plan has been set, it should never be adjusted regardless of what unexpected challenges, new information or changing requirements arise during the project's delivery.
toLevel2.correct: False
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Plans are adapted in response to unexpected challenges and changing requirements; fixed plans that ignore new information typically fail.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Adaptation to new information is standard project-management practice.

toLevel2.question: Communication with stakeholders — progress reports, updates, check-ins — is a part of project management that supports shared understanding and decision-making throughout delivery.
toLevel2.correct: True
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Communication supports shared understanding and decision-making; it is integral to project management.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Communication is integral to project management alongside planning and delivery.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes a project management plan?
toLevel2.correct: A document setting out objectives, deliverables, tasks, timelines, resources and responsibilities for creating a designed solution
toLevel2.correctExplanation: A plan integrates multiple elements to guide project delivery.

distractors.answer: A single list of materials needed for the project, with no reference to tasks, timelines, responsibilities or objectives across any phase of the project in any field
distractors.explanation: A materials list alone is insufficient; a plan integrates multiple elements.

distractors.answer: A promotional brochure designed to market the project to potential clients and partners after completion, with no content relevant to managing the project itself during delivery
distractors.explanation: A brochure is a marketing document, not a management plan.

distractors.answer: A legal contract between the designer and client specifying payment terms and deliverables but including no internal planning content for the project delivery team at any stage
distractors.explanation: A contract differs from an internal management plan.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes a milestone in project management?
toLevel2.correct: A checkpoint marking the completion of a defined deliverable or phase, used to track progress and inform next steps in the project
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Milestones mark defined completion points and inform next steps.

distractors.answer: A routine daily task that every team member performs regardless of whether a deliverable or phase has reached completion at any point during the project's duration
distractors.explanation: Routine daily tasks differ from milestones; milestones are checkpoints.

distractors.answer: A celebratory event held at the end of the project to mark its conclusion with food and speeches for all team members and stakeholders gathered at the venue
distractors.explanation: A celebration is not a milestone in the management sense.

distractors.answer: A mandatory training session run weekly across the duration of the project for all team members regardless of project stage or deliverable in progress at the time
distractors.explanation: A training session is not a milestone; milestones mark specific completions.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes a Gantt chart?
toLevel2.correct: A visual schedule showing tasks as horizontal bars along a timeline, typically indicating dependencies and milestones across the project
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Gantt charts are timeline visualisations with bars and dependencies.

distractors.answer: A pie chart showing the proportion of budget allocated to each task in the project over its full duration without any reference to task timing or dependencies at any stage
distractors.explanation: A pie chart shows proportions, not timelines; this is a different visualisation.

distractors.answer: A flow diagram representing decision points in a process with no timeline, duration or scheduling information attached to any of the nodes or arrows in the diagram
distractors.explanation: Flow diagrams show decisions rather than schedules.

distractors.answer: A spreadsheet listing every risk in the project without any timeline or task-duration information attached to the entries across the full duration of the project work schedule
distractors.explanation: A risk register is a different document from a Gantt chart.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes risk assessment in a project plan?
toLevel2.correct: Identifying potential problems, assessing their likelihood and impact, and outlining responses to reduce or manage them if they occur
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Risk assessment combines identification, assessment and planned response.

distractors.answer: Assuming no problems will occur during the project and therefore omitting any contingency planning from the management plan regardless of project complexity or duration
distractors.explanation: Risk assessment identifies potential problems rather than assuming none.

distractors.answer: Listing every imaginable catastrophe without any assessment of likelihood or impact and without any response plan attached to any of the listed risks in the document
distractors.explanation: Risk assessment includes assessment and response, not only listing.

distractors.answer: Transferring all project risk to an insurance company without any internal identification, assessment or response planning across any stage of the project delivery
distractors.explanation: Insurance is one response; internal identification and planning are also needed.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes resource allocation in project management?
toLevel2.correct: Assigning time, people, money and materials to specific tasks so that each task has the inputs required for completion on schedule
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Resource allocation matches inputs to tasks for completion on schedule.

distractors.answer: Distributing resources randomly across tasks without any reference to task requirements or project schedule across any phase of the project's delivery in practice
distractors.explanation: Random distribution is not allocation; allocation matches resources to task needs.

distractors.answer: Concentrating all resources on a single task regardless of the schedule or needs of other tasks that must also reach completion within the project's overall timeline
distractors.explanation: Concentration on one task neglects others; allocation spans the project.

distractors.answer: Withholding resources until project completion so that delivery occurs without any use of time, people, money or materials across the duration of the project's scheduled work
distractors.explanation: Withholding all resources prevents delivery; allocation makes them available.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes a project budget?
toLevel2.correct: A planned distribution of money or value units across tasks, resources and contingencies that guides spending across the project's life
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Budgets plan distribution across tasks, resources and contingencies.

distractors.answer: A single lump sum transferred at project completion with no prior planning about how it will be distributed across tasks or contingencies during delivery of the work involved
distractors.explanation: A lump sum is not a planned budget; planning occurs before and during delivery.

distractors.answer: A personal savings goal the designer sets before the project starts with no reference to project tasks, resources or contingencies across any phase of the delivery work
distractors.explanation: A personal savings goal is not a project budget.

distractors.answer: A fixed daily rate paid to the designer throughout the project with no allocation to specific tasks, resources or contingencies across the project's scheduled delivery time
distractors.explanation: A daily rate is compensation, not a project budget.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes task dependency in project scheduling?
toLevel2.correct: A relationship in which one task cannot begin or finish until another reaches a specified point in its own progress along the schedule
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Dependencies constrain start or finish of tasks relative to others.

distractors.answer: A relationship in which two tasks must always run in parallel regardless of their relative progress or the schedule of the project they belong to at any stage of delivery
distractors.explanation: Dependency is relational rather than requiring parallel operation.

distractors.answer: A relationship in which two tasks must always be completed by the same person regardless of the team's size or skills or the schedule of the project they are part of
distractors.explanation: Dependency concerns sequence rather than personnel assignment.

distractors.answer: A relationship in which two tasks must always use identical resources regardless of their nature or the schedule of the project they appear in at any stage of delivery
distractors.explanation: Dependency is about sequence, not resource identity.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes post-project review?
toLevel2.correct: A structured reflection on what worked and what to improve, capturing lessons for transfer to future projects after delivery is complete
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Post-project review extracts lessons for future transfer.

distractors.answer: A final quality check conducted on the delivered artefact by an external auditor without any engagement with the team about process or lessons for future projects afterwards
distractors.explanation: Quality check on the artefact is distinct from team reflection on process.

distractors.answer: A sales presentation to potential future clients showcasing the delivered outcome without any internal reflection on process or lessons across the course of the project delivery
distractors.explanation: A sales presentation is external promotion rather than internal review.

distractors.answer: A legal audit of compliance with contractual obligations without any team engagement with process or lessons for future projects across any part of the review session held
distractors.explanation: A legal audit is compliance-focused rather than a team-learning review.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes a quality requirement in a project plan?
toLevel2.correct: A specification of the standard a deliverable must meet — tolerance, material property, performance, user experience — attached to specific deliverables
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Quality requirements define standards across specific deliverables.

distractors.answer: A subjective impression the designer has about the deliverable without any specified standard, tolerance or measure attached to any deliverable in the project documentation
distractors.explanation: Quality requirements are specified standards, not subjective impressions alone.

distractors.answer: A fixed rating given to the team regardless of the deliverable's actual characteristics or the standards applied to its design and delivery across the project's duration
distractors.explanation: Quality requirements address deliverables rather than team ratings.

distractors.answer: A promotional claim used in marketing materials without any specification that deliverables actually meet any standard across any phase of the project's delivery cycle
distractors.explanation: Marketing claims differ from specified quality requirements.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes stakeholders in a design project?
toLevel2.correct: People affected by or contributing to the project — team members, end users, clients, teachers, community — whose interests and input matter to its progress
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Stakeholders span the range of people affected by or contributing to the project.

distractors.answer: Only the paying clients of the project, with no role for team members, end users, teachers or community whose interests the project may affect across delivery
distractors.explanation: Stakeholders extend beyond paying clients to team, users and community.

distractors.answer: Only the designer leading the project, with no role for any other person, organisation or community whose interests the project may affect at any point during delivery
distractors.explanation: Stakeholders include many parties beyond the lead designer.

distractors.answer: Only the external auditors reviewing compliance at the end of the project with no role during delivery or input from other parties whose interests the project may affect
distractors.explanation: Auditors are one category; many others are stakeholders across the project lifecycle.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes plan adaptation during project delivery?
toLevel2.correct: Adjusting objectives, tasks, timelines or resources in response to unexpected challenges, new information or changing requirements encountered during delivery
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Adaptation responds to change during delivery by adjusting plan elements.

distractors.answer: Refusing any change to the plan regardless of what unexpected challenges, new information or changing requirements arise during the project's delivery across any stage
distractors.explanation: Refusing adaptation typically leads to project failure; adjustment is standard.

distractors.answer: Replacing the entire plan with a new one every week regardless of the scale or nature of any change encountered across the project's delivery over its full duration
distractors.explanation: Weekly wholesale replacement is not adaptation; targeted adjustment is.

distractors.answer: Abandoning the project entirely whenever any unexpected challenge arises during delivery regardless of the challenge's scale or the options available for response in the situation
distractors.explanation: Abandonment is rarely the response to challenges; adaptation is.

toLevel2.question: Which one of the following best describes stakeholder communication in a project?
toLevel2.correct: Regular updates, check-ins and progress reports that keep stakeholders informed and support shared understanding and decision-making during delivery
toLevel2.correctExplanation: Stakeholder communication supports shared understanding and decision-making.

distractors.answer: A single meeting at the project's start with no further communication with stakeholders across any phase of delivery regardless of the project's length or complexity encountered
distractors.explanation: A single start meeting is insufficient; regular communication supports delivery.

distractors.answer: Silent delivery in which stakeholders receive no updates, reports or check-ins across the project's delivery regardless of its length, complexity or stakeholder needs involved
distractors.explanation: Silent delivery undermines shared understanding and decision-making.

distractors.answer: Uninvited surprise communications at random times with content unrelated to the project's progress or to the stakeholders' interests across any phase of the delivery work undertaken
distractors.explanation: Random unrelated communication does not support project purposes.

toLevel2.sentence: A specific checkpoint marking completion of a defined deliverable or phase is a project {{blank:1}}; a visual schedule showing tasks as horizontal bars along a timeline is a {{blank:2}} chart.

blanks.correctExplanation: Milestones mark defined completion points.

distractors.answer: stakeholder
distractors.explanation: Stakeholders are people, not checkpoints.

distractors.answer: invoice
distractors.explanation: Invoices are financial documents rather than checkpoints.

blanks.correctExplanation: Gantt charts are the specific timeline visualisation named.

distractors.answer: Venn
distractors.explanation: Venn diagrams show set relationships, not schedules.

distractors.answer: pie
distractors.explanation: Pie charts show proportions, not timelines.

toLevel2.sentence: The process of identifying potential problems, assessing their likelihood and impact, and planning responses is {{blank:1}} assessment; a structured reflection after delivery to capture lessons for future projects is post-project {{blank:2}}.

blanks.correctExplanation: Risk assessment is the process described.

distractors.answer: reward
distractors.explanation: Reward is not the focus of the identified process.

distractors.answer: resource
distractors.explanation: Resource assessment addresses inputs rather than potential problems.

blanks.correctExplanation: Post-project review captures lessons for future transfer.

distractors.answer: repair
distractors.explanation: Repair is not the reflective term used.

distractors.answer: rehearsal
distractors.explanation: Rehearsal occurs before performance rather than after delivery.

toLevel3.question: Plans that treat all tasks as equally important always outperform plans that identify critical-path tasks, because equal treatment produces the most balanced project delivery.
toLevel3.correct: False
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Plans that identify critical-path tasks typically outperform equal-treatment plans because delays on critical-path tasks push out completion; focused attention improves delivery.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Critical-path identification supports better delivery than equal-treatment scheduling.

toLevel3.question: A project plan that omits any time for iteration, testing and refinement reliably delivers the quality specified in the brief because skilled designers produce correct work on the first attempt.
toLevel3.correct: False
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Plans that omit iteration, testing and refinement typically fail to deliver specified quality; complex problems rarely yield to first-attempt work even from skilled designers.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: First-attempt delivery without iteration typically fails specified quality; iteration is needed.

toLevel3.question: Sharing project progress with stakeholders on a regular cadence is unnecessary because stakeholders always learn what they need to know from the final delivery at the end of the project.
toLevel3.correct: False
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Stakeholders benefit from regular updates; waiting for final delivery undermines shared decision-making and responsiveness across the project.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Regular updates support shared decision-making; final-only communication undermines it.

toLevel3.question: Buffer time built into a schedule absorbs unexpected delay without pushing the critical path out, which supports delivery when risks materialise during the project.
toLevel3.correct: True
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Buffers absorb unexpected delay, protecting the critical path and supporting on-time delivery.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Buffers protect the critical path from pushed-out completion.

toLevel3.question: A risk log that is created at the start of the project and never updated provides the same value across the project's delivery as a log that is reviewed and updated regularly.
toLevel3.correct: False
toLevel3.correctExplanation: A risk log requires regular review and update to remain useful; static logs lose relevance as conditions change through delivery.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Risk logs require updating; static logs lose relevance as conditions change.

toLevel3.question: Team projects benefit from clear role definition so that team members know what each person is responsible for, reducing gaps and overlap that otherwise consume effort.
toLevel3.correct: True
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Clear roles reduce coordination overhead by preventing gaps and overlap in work assignment.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Clear roles support coordination; ambiguity increases effort through gaps and overlap.

toLevel3.question: A project plan is a communication tool as well as a scheduling tool, helping team members, clients and other stakeholders develop a shared understanding of the project.
toLevel3.correct: True
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Plans communicate intentions alongside scheduling, supporting shared understanding across stakeholders.

distractors.answer: False
distractors.explanation: Plans communicate alongside scheduling; communication is integral to their function.

toLevel3.question: Project management software can coordinate team activities but has no role in recording decisions, tracking progress or supporting communication among team members during delivery.
toLevel3.correct: False
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Project management software typically supports decision recording, progress tracking and communication alongside activity coordination.

distractors.answer: True
distractors.explanation: Project management software supports multiple functions beyond coordination alone.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why identifying the critical path supports effective project management?
toLevel3.correct: Tasks on the critical path have no buffer, so delays on them push out overall completion; identifying them focuses attention where schedule risk is greatest
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Critical-path focus directs attention where schedule risk is greatest.

distractors.answer: Critical path identification is irrelevant to project management because all tasks are equally important regardless of their position in the schedule across any project in any field
distractors.explanation: Critical-path tasks concentrate schedule risk; they are not equally important to overall delivery.

distractors.answer: Critical path identification determines payment rates for team members based on whether their tasks sit on the critical path of the project schedule during any phase of delivery
distractors.explanation: Payment rates are not determined by critical-path membership; the function is schedule management.

distractors.answer: Critical path identification requires the team to complete all tasks on the critical path before beginning any other work regardless of resource availability across the project team
distractors.explanation: Critical-path focus directs attention rather than requiring strict task sequencing of this kind.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why plans include buffer time in their schedules?
toLevel3.correct: Buffers absorb unexpected delay without pushing the critical path out, supporting reliable delivery when risks materialise during the project
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Buffers support delivery reliability under materialising risks.

distractors.answer: Buffers are unnecessary because plans assume every task will complete exactly on schedule without any delay across any phase of the project regardless of its complexity or duration
distractors.explanation: Tasks do delay; buffers absorb these.

distractors.answer: Buffers are required to extend the project artificially so that team members can avoid completing work on the expected schedule across the full duration of the project in any field
distractors.explanation: Buffers absorb delay rather than enable deliberate delay.

distractors.answer: Buffers are placed randomly in the schedule without any reference to the critical path or the tasks most likely to delay the project's completion across any phase of delivery
distractors.explanation: Buffers are placed deliberately in relation to risk and the critical path.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why a risk log requires regular review and update?
toLevel3.correct: Conditions change across the project — new information, altered circumstances, resolved or emerged risks — and update keeps the log accurate and actionable
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Regular review keeps the log relevant to current conditions and actionable.

distractors.answer: Risk logs are static documents and require no update regardless of project conditions or changes encountered during delivery across any phase of the project's full duration
distractors.explanation: Static logs lose relevance; regular update maintains usefulness.

distractors.answer: Risk logs are maintained solely for legal compliance and have no role in project management decisions regardless of the conditions encountered during the project's delivery
distractors.explanation: Risk logs support management decisions alongside any compliance role.

distractors.answer: Risk logs are reviewed only at project close with no role during delivery regardless of the project's length, complexity or the risks actually encountered by the team in delivery
distractors.explanation: Review across delivery supports response to risks as they arise.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains how role definition supports team coordination?
toLevel3.correct: Clear roles reduce gaps and overlap in work assignment, so team members know what they are responsible for and coordinate efficiently across the project
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Role clarity reduces gaps and overlap that would otherwise consume coordination effort.

distractors.answer: Role definition has no effect on team coordination because gaps and overlap in work assignment are irrelevant to project delivery across any field of design practice at any level
distractors.explanation: Gaps and overlap substantially affect delivery; role definition addresses them.

distractors.answer: Role definition requires every team member to have identical responsibilities regardless of their skills, experience or the project's specific needs across any phase of delivery
distractors.explanation: Role definition allocates differentiated responsibility rather than uniform assignment.

distractors.answer: Role definition is prohibited in contemporary team practice across Australian design education so students are required to work without defined roles at every year level today
distractors.explanation: No such prohibition exists; role definition is standard practice.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why stakeholder communication follows a regular cadence rather than waiting for final delivery?
toLevel3.correct: Regular communication supports shared understanding, informed decision-making and responsive action across the project rather than only at its conclusion
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Regular cadence supports responsive management and informed decisions across the project.

distractors.answer: Final-only communication always produces stronger outcomes because stakeholders only need to know what was delivered at the end of the project regardless of complexity encountered
distractors.explanation: Final-only communication undermines responsive management across the project.

distractors.answer: Regular cadence is a waste of time because stakeholders have no role during delivery and only engage with the final outcome at project close across any field of design
distractors.explanation: Stakeholders have substantive roles during delivery alongside final outcome.

distractors.answer: Stakeholder communication is prohibited under Australian design-education policy so students are required to deliver projects without any communication until the final day today
distractors.explanation: No such prohibition exists; regular communication is encouraged.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why project management software supports team work beyond activity coordination alone?
toLevel3.correct: Software records decisions, tracks progress against schedule, supports communication between team members and preserves history across the project's delivery
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Software supports decisions, progress, communication and history beyond coordination alone.

distractors.answer: Project management software is a scheduling tool only with no role for decisions, progress tracking or communication across any design project in any field or scale of work
distractors.explanation: Software supports multiple functions beyond scheduling.

distractors.answer: Project management software replaces all team members so that no human work is required across the delivery of any project regardless of complexity, scale or field
distractors.explanation: Software supports rather than replaces team work.

distractors.answer: Project management software is prohibited in contemporary design education across Australia so students manage projects without any digital tools at every year level today
distractors.explanation: No such prohibition exists; software is widely used in practice and education.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why plans are adapted rather than rigidly followed during delivery?
toLevel3.correct: Unexpected challenges, new information and changing requirements arise across delivery, and adaptation keeps the plan useful as conditions evolve
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Adaptation keeps the plan useful as conditions evolve through delivery.

distractors.answer: Rigid following of a plan always produces better outcomes regardless of unexpected challenges, new information or changing requirements across any project in any design field today
distractors.explanation: Rigid following under changing conditions produces worse outcomes; adaptation is standard.

distractors.answer: Plans are replaced entirely every week regardless of the scale of change encountered or the specific challenges involved in delivery across the project's full duration in practice
distractors.explanation: Weekly wholesale replacement is not adaptation; targeted adjustment is.

distractors.answer: Plans are adapted only when team members disagree among themselves regardless of the actual project challenges or conditions encountered during delivery across any phase of work
distractors.explanation: Adaptation responds to conditions, not only team disagreement.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why iteration time supports quality delivery?
toLevel3.correct: Iteration allows testing, refinement and correction in response to findings that emerge only through making, supporting delivery to specified quality
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Iteration supports quality through testing, refinement and correction.

distractors.answer: Iteration time wastes schedule that should be used on first-attempt production across every project regardless of complexity, risk or quality requirements in the brief at any level
distractors.explanation: First-attempt production without iteration rarely meets specified quality in complex projects.

distractors.answer: Iteration is prohibited in contemporary project management across Australian design education so students must deliver first-attempt outcomes at every year level of study today
distractors.explanation: Iteration is standard in design practice and education.

distractors.answer: Iteration is required only for failed projects and adds no value to successful projects regardless of the complexity or quality requirements across any design field observed in practice
distractors.explanation: Iteration contributes to successful projects alongside any corrective role in troubled ones.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why post-project review produces value for future projects?
toLevel3.correct: Review captures what worked, what did not and why, in transferable form that can inform planning, delivery and team practice on subsequent projects
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Transferable lessons support future planning, delivery and team practice.

distractors.answer: Post-project review produces no value for future projects because each project is entirely unique with no transferable lessons across any field at any level of practice in design
distractors.explanation: Transferable lessons exist across many projects; unique-project framing misses this.

distractors.answer: Post-project review is a legal requirement only with no contribution to team practice or future project planning across any field of design work observed in contemporary industry today
distractors.explanation: Review supports practice and planning alongside any legal role.

distractors.answer: Post-project review is conducted only by external consultants with no team engagement and has no value for team members' future work across any project or field at any level
distractors.explanation: Team engagement in review is central to producing transferable lessons for the team.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why budget and schedule interact in project management?
toLevel3.correct: Additional time often requires additional cost, and cost pressures shape what can be delivered in the available time, so tracking both together is essential
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Time and cost interact; tracking both together supports delivery decisions.

distractors.answer: Budget and schedule operate in entirely separate domains with no interaction across any project regardless of its scale, complexity or field of design practice involved at any level
distractors.explanation: Time and cost interact substantively; separate-domain framing misses this.

distractors.answer: Budget has no effect on schedule and schedule has no effect on budget across any project in any field at any level of professional practice in design today globally
distractors.explanation: They substantially affect each other.

distractors.answer: Budget and schedule are identical concepts and tracking one tracks the other automatically across every project in every field of design practice in contemporary industry at any level
distractors.explanation: They are distinct concepts with overlapping but not identical implications.

toLevel3.question: Which one of the following best explains why project plans adapt to different scales — individual, team, collaborative?
toLevel3.correct: Different scales involve different coordination, communication and accountability needs, so plans adapt elements such as role definition and communication cadence accordingly
toLevel3.correctExplanation: Scale drives coordination, communication and accountability needs, shaping plan elements.
